---
title: "INCISE"
subtitle: "Integration of citizen science and standardised survey data to assess biodiversity dynamics"
description: "INCISE project: integrating citizen science and structured monitoring data to improve estimates of species distributions and population trends. H2020-MSCA-COFUND UNA4CAREER fellowship."
toc: true
---

## The Challenge

Reliable estimates of species' distributions and population trends are essential for conservation, but the data we use to build them come with important limitations. Standardised monitoring schemes provide high-quality data but are limited in spatial and temporal coverage. Citizen science platforms like [eBird](https://ebird.org) generate massive amounts of data, but with uneven effort and known biases.

Can we combine the best of both worlds?

## What We Did

INCISE used local structured field survey data to develop reliable baseline estimates of species' distribution and population dynamics, then explored the advantages of integrating these with citizen science data into a single analytical framework. The project aimed to:

- **Identify and quantify biases** in citizen science data relative to structured surveys
- **Develop integrated models** that combine both data types to improve inference
- **Provide practical guidelines** for producing more reliable species trend and range assessments

## Funding & Context

- **Programme**: H2020-MSCA-COFUND-2018 · [UNA4CAREER](https://www.una4career.eu/) fellowship
- **Reference**: PR17/24
- **Period**: 2022--2023 (resigned after one year upon obtaining the UCM position)
- **Role**: Principal Investigator
- **Host**: Universidad Complutense de Madrid

## Related Work

The data integration approach developed during INCISE continues to inform my current work on [biodiversity monitoring and modelling](../research/forecasting.qmd), where combining heterogeneous data sources remains a central challenge.
